title: Mellon Financial

infobox.value: Merged with [Bank of New York](/wiki/Bank_of_New_York "Bank of New York") to become [BNY Mellon](/wiki/The_Bank_of_New_York_Mellon "The Bank of New York Mellon")
[The Bank of New York Mellon](/wiki/The_Bank_of_New_York_Mellon "The Bank of New York Mellon")

was an American investment firm which was once one of the world's largest money management firms. Based in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), Pennsylvania, it was in the business of institutional and [high-net-worth individual](/wiki/High-net-worth_individual "High-net-worth individual") asset management, including the Dreyfus family of [mutual funds](/wiki/Mutual_fund "Mutual fund"), business [banking](/wiki/Banking "Banking"), and [shareholder](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder") and [investor](/wiki/Investor "Investor") services. On December 4, 2006, it announced a [merger](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") agreement with [Bank of New York](/wiki/Bank_of_New_York "Bank of New York"), to form [BNY Mellon](/wiki/BNY "BNY"). After regulatory and shareholder approval, the banks completed the merger on July 2, 2007.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Former investment firm located in the US

Mellon Financial Corporation

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Mellon](/wiki/File:Mellonlogo.svg "Mellon") | |
| Industry | [Financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") |
| Founded | January 2, 1870; 156 years ago (1870-01-02)[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) |
| Founder | [Thomas Mellon](/wiki/Thomas_Mellon "Thomas Mellon") |
| Defunct | July 2, 2007 (2007-07-02) |
| Fate | Merged with [Bank of New York](/wiki/Bank_of_New_York "Bank of New York") to become [BNY Mellon](/wiki/The_Bank_of_New_York_Mellon "The Bank of New York Mellon") |
| Successor | [The Bank of New York Mellon](/wiki/The_Bank_of_New_York_Mellon "The Bank of New York Mellon") |
| Headquarters | [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), Pennsylvania, U.S. |
| Website | [mellon.com](https://web.archive.org/web/20070630123606/https://www.mellon.com/) |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Pittsburgh-mellon-center-2007-night.jpg/250px-Pittsburgh-mellon-center-2007-night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pittsburgh-mellon-center-2007-night.jpg)

[One Mellon Center](/wiki/One_Mellon_Center "One Mellon Center"), the corporate headquarters in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), at night

**Mellon Financial Corporation** was an American investment firm which was once one of the world's largest money management firms. Based in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), Pennsylvania, it was in the business of institutional and [high-net-worth individual](/wiki/High-net-worth_individual "High-net-worth individual") asset management, including the Dreyfus family of [mutual funds](/wiki/Mutual_fund "Mutual fund"), business [banking](/wiki/Banking "Banking"), and [shareholder](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder") and [investor](/wiki/Investor "Investor") services. On December 4, 2006, it announced a [merger](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") agreement with [Bank of New York](/wiki/Bank_of_New_York "Bank of New York"), to form [BNY Mellon](/wiki/BNY "BNY"). After regulatory and shareholder approval, the banks completed the merger on July 2, 2007.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mellon_Financial&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Mellon was opened in January 1870 by [Thomas Mellon](/wiki/Thomas_Mellon "Thomas Mellon") and his sons [Andrew Mellon](/wiki/Andrew_Mellon "Andrew Mellon") and [Richard B. Mellon](/wiki/Richard_B._Mellon "Richard B. Mellon"), as T. Mellon & Sons' Bank. In 1902, the institution became Mellon National Bank.

Mellon Bank was an important force in the [mass production revolution](/wiki/Mass_production#History "Mass production") in the United States, especially in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Mellon family using the bank as a proxy had direct involvement with founding the modern aluminium, oil, consumer electronics and financial industries. [Alcoa](/wiki/Alcoa "Alcoa"), [Gulf Oil](/wiki/Gulf_Oil "Gulf Oil") (now Chevron-Texaco), [Westinghouse](/wiki/Westinghouse_Electric_Corporation_(1886) "Westinghouse Electric Corporation (1886)") (now [CBS Corporation](/wiki/CBS_Corporation "CBS Corporation") and [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens")) and [Rockwell](/wiki/Rockwell_International "Rockwell International"), all were directly founded and managed by the bank. [U.S. Steel](/wiki/U.S._Steel "U.S. Steel") (the world's first billion-dollar corporation), [Heinz](/wiki/H._J._Heinz_Company "H. J. Heinz Company"), [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), [Koppers](/wiki/Koppers "Koppers") and [ExxonMobil](/wiki/ExxonMobil "ExxonMobil") (as Rockefeller's Standard Oil) were born and nurtured by Mellon.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* In 1920, Andrew left his leadership post of the bank to become the longest serving [U.S. Treasury Secretary](/wiki/U.S._Treasury_Secretary "U.S. Treasury Secretary") in history (serving under three separate administrations).
* In 1929, Richard founded Mellbank Corporation. In 1946, Mellon National, Mellbank, and the Union Trust Company merged to form Mellon National Bank and Trust Company.
* A reorganization in 1972 brought about a name change to Mellon Bank, N.A. and the formation of a holding company, Mellon National Corporation.
* In 1983, Mellon bought [Girard Bank](/wiki/Girard_Bank "Girard Bank") of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and Central Counties Bank of [State College, Pennsylvania](/wiki/State_College,_Pennsylvania "State College, Pennsylvania"). The next year, Mellon National Corporation became Mellon Bank Corporation, and purchased Northwest Pennsylvania Corporation of [Oil City, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Oil_City,_Pennsylvania "Oil City, Pennsylvania").
* In 1986, Mellon bought Commonwealth National Financial of [Harrisburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania"). It is also reported that Mellon operated the 2nd largest financial computing system in the world.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* In 1991, Mellon bought United Penn Bank of [Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Wilkes-Barre,_Pennsylvania "Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania"). The next year, Mellon bought 54 branch offices of Philadelphia-based [Philadelphia Savings Fund Society](/wiki/Philadelphia_Savings_Fund_Society "Philadelphia Savings Fund Society"), whose parent company had become insolvent. Philadelphia Savings Fund Society, was the first savings bank in the United States, founded in 1819.
* In 1996, Mellon joined with [CIBC](/wiki/CIBC "CIBC") to found [CIBC Mellon](/wiki/CIBC_Mellon "CIBC Mellon") Global Securities Services (CMGSS) in a 50-50 joint venture.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/Boston_Company_1993_logo.png/250px-Boston_Company_1993_logo.png)](/wiki/File:Boston_Company_1993_logo.png)

  The Boston Company logo, c. 1993, acquired by Mellon

   In 1993, Mellon bought The Boston Company from [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express") and AFCO Credit Corporation from The Continental Corporation. The next year, Mellon merged with the [Dreyfus Corporation](/wiki/Dreyfus_Corporation "Dreyfus Corporation"), bringing its mutual funds under its umbrella.
* 1998 saw Mellon's purchase of United Bankshares, Inc., of [Miami](/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida"), 1st Business Bank of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California"), and Founders Asset Management.
* In 1999, Martin G. McGuinn became chairman and chief executive officer of Mellon Bank Corporation. Mellon Bank Corporation then became Mellon Financial Corporation. Two years later, it sold its [retail banking](/wiki/Retail_banking "Retail banking") operations to [Citizens Financial Group](/wiki/Citizens_Financial_Group "Citizens Financial Group").
* In 2004, Mellon announced it would purchase Safeco Trust Company from [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle")-based [Safeco Corporation](/wiki/Safeco_Corporation "Safeco Corporation"). The same year, it purchased outstanding shares in [London](/wiki/London "London")-based [Pareto Partners](/w/index.php?title=Pareto_Partners&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pareto Partners (page does not exist)") and offered them floor space in [Mellon Financial Center](/wiki/Mellon_Financial_Center "Mellon Financial Center") (opened earlier in the year).

## Merger with Bank of New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mellon_Financial&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Merger with Bank of New York")]

Main article: [BNY Mellon](/wiki/BNY_Mellon "BNY Mellon")

In 2006, Mellon announced its plans to merge with Bank of New York. Talks began when [Tom Renyi](/wiki/Tom_Renyi "Tom Renyi") approached Robert Kelly about a possible amalgamation between the Bank of New York and Mellon Financial Corporation.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT-5) The [$](/wiki/United_States_Dollar "United States Dollar")16.5 billion deal ($26.4 billion today) was announced in December 2006 and finalized on July 1, 2007, with Kelly as the [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") (CEO) of the new company, and Renyi as executive chairman.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Per the deal, the new [Board of Directors](/wiki/Board_of_Directors "Board of Directors") is composed of ten directors appointed by the Bank of New York, and eight by Mellon.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The merger was completed July 1, 2007, as [The Bank of New York Mellon](/wiki/The_Bank_of_New_York_Mellon "The Bank of New York Mellon"). Headquartered in New York, it is the world's largest securities servicing firm and one of the world's top ten asset managing firms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The new venture launched its brand identity on October 1, 2007.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

These two companies, along with [State Street](/wiki/State_Street_Corporation "State Street Corporation"), followed essentially the same evolution. All were originally large diversified financial service providers, particularly in the corporate banking space in the regions they were located in. However, competition in the corporate loans and retail banking businesses saw them jettison these operations in favor of what were believed to be more stable, fee based business: [asset management](/wiki/Asset_management "Asset management") (i.e. [investment management](/wiki/Investment_management "Investment management") in the form of mutual funds and other [separately managed accounts](/wiki/Separately_managed_account "Separately managed account")) and asset servicing (i.e. [corporate trust](/wiki/Corporate_trust "Corporate trust"), [stock transfer services](/w/index.php?title=Stock_transfer_services&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stock transfer services (page does not exist)") and [depository receipts](/w/index.php?title=Depository_receipts&action=edit&redlink=1 "Depository receipts (page does not exist)")).

Mellon is a large provider of what are known as controlled disbursement accounts. These are checking accounts in specialized locations which are given early warning by the Federal Reserve as to what checks will be clearing them. Companies can then transfer the exact amount needed to pay those checks, while then investing the unneeded money or using other funds to pay down debt.

## Mellon Bank CEOs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mellon_Financial&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mellon Bank CEOs")]

* [Frank R. Denton](/w/index.php?title=Frank_R._Denton&action=edit&redlink=1 "Frank R. Denton (page does not exist)") (1946 – February 8, 1963)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [John A. Mayer](/w/index.php?title=John_A._Mayer&action=edit&redlink=1 "John A. Mayer (page does not exist)") (February 8, 1963 – August 1, 1974)[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [James H. Higgins](/w/index.php?title=James_H._Higgins_(business_executive)&action=edit&redlink=1 "James H. Higgins (business executive) (page does not exist)") (August 1, 1974[[12]](#cite_note-12) – March 1, 1981)[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [J. David Barnes](/w/index.php?title=J._David_Barnes&action=edit&redlink=1 "J. David Barnes (page does not exist)") (March 1, 1981 – April 13, 1987)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Frank Cahouet](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Cahouet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Frank Cahouet (page does not exist)") (April 13, 1987 – January 1, 1999)
* [Martin G. McGuinn](/w/index.php?title=Martin_G._McGuinn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Martin G. McGuinn (page does not exist)") (January 1, 1999 – February 13, 2006)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* Robert E. Kelly (February 13, 2006 – July 1, 2007)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
